concept.headline: Vec<T> is Rust's growable list — heap-allocated, dynamically sized, single type.
concept.explanation: A Vec<T> stores a sequence of T values on the heap. Unlike arrays (fixed at compile time), Vecs can grow and shrink at runtime. Common operations: push to append, pop to remove the last, [] to index (panics out-of-bounds), and .get(i) for safe Option access. Iterating with for x in &v borrows each element immutably.
keyPoints: Vec stores all elements of the same type T
push/pop operate at the end — O(1) amortised
v[i] panics if i >= v.len(); v.get(i) returns Option<&T>
for x in &v borrows (read-only); for x in &mut v borrows mutably; for x in v consumes
v.iter().map().filter().sum() — iterator adapters work on Vec slices

demo.intro: Watch a Vec grow with push, then be iterated and summed.

explanation.why: Vec::new() allocates a heap buffer with zero length and some initial capacity
explanation.intent: Prepare a growable list for collecting scores
explanation.misconception: Vec::new() does NOT allocate heap memory immediately — it creates a zero-capacity handle. Heap allocation occurs on the first push.

explanation.why: push moves the value onto the heap buffer; if capacity is exceeded, Rust reallocates with doubled capacity
explanation.intent: Add the first score to the list
explanation.misconception: push takes ownership of the value (here i32 is Copy, so it copies). For non-Copy types, the Vec owns them after push.

explanation.why: Each push appends to the end of the buffer
explanation.intent: Grow the list with a second element
explanation.misconception: push always appends to the end — there is no insert at beginning in O(1)

explanation.why: Third push; now the Vec has 3 elements
explanation.misconception: The indices are 0, 1, 2 — the last valid index is scores.len() - 1 = 2

explanation.why: len() returns the number of elements currently stored
explanation.intent: Confirm the Vec has grown to 3 elements
explanation.misconception: len() is O(1) — the Vec stores its length as metadata, not by counting elements

explanation.why: Index 0 accesses the first element; i32 is Copy so it is copied out
explanation.intent: Show direct element access by index
explanation.misconception: scores[0] would panic if scores were empty — len() checked above confirms it is not

explanation.why: iter() creates a borrowing iterator over &i32 elements; sum() accumulates them into an i32
explanation.intent: Demonstrate iterator chaining on a Vec
explanation.misconception: iter() does not consume the Vec — it borrows. You can use scores again after this line.
The type annotation `let total: i32` is required because sum() is generic and needs to know what type to accumulate into

explanation.what: for s in &scores — immutable borrow iteration
explanation.why: &scores creates a borrowing iterator; s is a &i32 reference to each element
explanation.intent: Print each element without consuming the Vec
explanation.misconception: for s in scores (without &) would move the Vec into the loop — scores would be unusable afterward

explanation.why: s dereferences to 85 automatically; {} auto-derefs the &i32
explanation.misconception: println!("{}", s) auto-derefs the &i32 reference — you do not need to write *s
The iterator is lazy — each call to next() yields the next element

explanation.why: Iterator reaches index 2 — the last element
explanation.misconception: After yielding 78, the iterator returns None and the for loop exits naturally

prediction.explanation: v.len() is 3. v.len() - 1 = 2. v[2] is the last element, 30. No panic — 2 is a valid index.

explanation.afterDemo: The Vec started empty, grew with three push calls, then we read from it by length, by index, via sum(), and by iteration — all without consuming it (we used &scores throughout). The Vec was dropped automatically when main exited.
keyTakeaways: Vec<T> grows dynamically on the heap; push appends, pop removes the last.
Use v.get(i) instead of v[i] when the index might be out of bounds — get returns Option.
for x in &v borrows each element; for x in v consumes the Vec.

practice.prompt: Create a Vec<i32> with values 2, 4, 6, 8. Print only the elements greater than 4 (one per line) using iter().filter().
practice.hint: v.iter().filter(|&&n| n > 4) produces references to elements where the value > 4.

feedback.successMessage: Correct! filter keeps only elements where n > 4, yielding 6 and 8.
retryHints: v.iter() yields &&i32 in the filter closure because iter() yields &i32 and filter passes &&T.
Pattern |&&n| destructures both layers of reference to get the i32 n.

summary.title: Vectors — Complete
takeaways: Vec<T> is the go-to growable collection in Rust.
Use v.get(i) for safe indexed access; v[i] panics on out-of-bounds.
Iterator adapters (.map(), .filter(), .sum()) work directly on Vec slices.
